name: error_distribution.html
content:
Error Distribution - Residuals Distribution versus Normal Curve Plot: An Error Distribution plot, typically presented as a Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) of the residuals overlaid with a Normal Distribution (Gaussian) curve, is used to visually assess the assumption of normality in the error terms. The vertical axis represents the density, while the horizontal axis represents the residual values.

For all models, the closer the KDE curve aligns with the Normal overlay, the more likely it is that the normality of residuals assumption holds, which is critical for the validity of t-tests and F-tests.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    Significant divergence from the Normal curve—such as skewness (asymmetry) or excess kurtosis (heavy tails)—indicates that the OLS estimators, while potentially remaining unbiased, may no longer be the most efficient. It also suggests that confidence intervals might be unreliable.

    The plot evaluates the distribution of raw residuals. If assumptions hold, they should follow \(N(0, \sigma^2)\): $$f(e_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-\frac{e_i^2}{2\sigma^2}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad e_i = y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for OLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    This plot evaluates the standardized (weighted) residuals. A successful WLS implementation should transform the residuals such that their distribution appears Gaussian. If the KDE remains highly non-normal, the weighting scheme may be incorrect or failing to handle the underlying heteroskedasticity.

    For WLS, we plot the density of the whitened (weighted) residuals. The weighting transformation ensures that the residuals should have a constant variance across the sample: $$f(\tilde{e}_i) \quad \text{vs.} \quad \tilde{e}_i = \sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{WLS})$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{WLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for WLS regression
    • \(w_i\) is the weight for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    The density curve is plotted for the transformed residuals after accounting for the covariance structure. Alignment with the Normal curve confirms that the GLS transformation has successfully isolated white noise from the previously observed correlated or heteroskedastic errors.

    GLS diagnostics use residuals whitened by the Cholesky factor (\(C\)) of the covariance matrix \(\Sigma \) to remove autocorrelation or heteroscedasticity: $$f(\mathbf{e}_{wh}) \quad \text{vs.} \quad \mathbf{e}_{wh} = \mathbf{C}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\beta}_{GLS})$$ Where:
    • \(\mathbf{y}\) is the vector of observed values
    • \(\mathbf{X}\) is the design matrix
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{GLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for GLS regression
    • \(\mathbf{C}\) is the Cholesky factor of the covariance matrix


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    The plot utilizes standardized recursive residuals. Because these residuals are assumed to be independent and identically distributed under the null hypothesis of parameter stability, a highly non-normal density curve often points toward structural breaks or model misspecification, occurring during the recursive estimation process.

    In RLS, we typically analyze the density of Recursive Residuals (\(v_{t}\)). These are particularly useful for detecting structural breaks or parameter instability: $$f(v_t) \quad \text{vs.} \quad v_t = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the actual observed value for time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor vector for time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients for time \(t-1\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot visualizes the aggregate distribution of residuals across all moving windows. If the KDE shows multiple peaks (multimodality) or extreme outliers compared to the Normal curve, it indicates that the error distribution is shifting across different time segments or regimes.

    Rolling residuals represent the error at time \(t\) relative to the parameters estimated within the local window \([t-w+1, t]\): $$f(e_{t, \text{roll}}) \quad \text{vs.} \quad e_{t, \text{roll}} = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the actual observed value for time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor vector for time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)

name: normal_q_q.html
content:
Normal Q-Q Plot: A Normal Q-Q (Quantile-Quantile) plot is a graphical diagnostic tool used to assess whether the residuals of a model follow a theoretical normal distribution. The plot displays the sample quantiles of the residuals on the vertical axis against the theoretical quantiles of a standard normal distribution on the horizontal axis.

In a well-specified model, the points should fall closely along a 45-degree reference line. Significant deviations from this line indicate departures from "normality".

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    Curvature at the ends of the line indicates heavy tails (outliers) or thin tails, while an overall S-shape suggests skewness. Such departures imply that p-values and confidence intervals derived from OLS may be inaccurate, particularly in small samples.

    The plot compares the sorted raw residuals against the expected values from a \(N(0, 1)\) distribution: $$Q(p) \text{ of } \{y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}\} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Phi^{-1}(p)$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for OLS regression


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    This plot utilizes standardized (weighted) residuals. If the weighting scheme has effectively neutralized heteroskedasticity, the resulting quantiles should align with the normal reference line. Persistent deviations suggest the weights did not correctly account for the variance structure.

    In WLS, the Q-Q plot uses whitened residuals (\(\tilde{e}_{i}\)). This is critical because the raw residuals are not expected to be identically distributed due to heteroscedasticity; only the weighted residuals should follow a normal distribution: $$Q(p) \text{ of } \{\sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{WLS})\} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Phi^{-1}(p)$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{WLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for WLS regression
    • \(w_i\) is the weight for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    The Q-Q plot assesses transformed residuals that have been adjusted for autocorrelation or complex covariance structure. Alignment with the line confirms that the GLS transformation has successfully reduced the errors to normally distributed white noise.

    Like WLS, GLS requires whitening using the Cholesky factor (\(C\)) to account for the correlation structure defined in the covariance matrix \(\Sigma \): $$Q(p) \text{ of } \{\mathbf{C}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\beta}_{GLS})\} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Phi^{-1}(p)$$ Where:
    • \(\mathbf{y}\) is the vector of observed values
    • \(\mathbf{X}\) is the design matrix
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{GLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for GLS regression
    • \(\mathbf{C}\) is the Cholesky factor of the covariance matrix


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    Standardized recursive residuals are plotted to verify that the innovations are Gaussian. Points drifting off the line during specific quantiles often signify structural breaks or parameter instability, occurring at specific points in the data sequence

    The Q-Q plot for RLS uses standardized recursive residuals (\(w_{t}\)). These are preferred because, under the null hypothesis of parameter stability, they are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) \(N(0, \sigma^2)\): $$Q(p) \text{ of } \left\{ \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}}{\sigma \sqrt{f_t}} \right\} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Phi^{-1}(p)$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the actual observed value for time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor vector for time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients for time \(t-1\)
    • \(f_t\) is the recursive weight for time \(t\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot evaluates the normality of residuals generated across moving windows. If the points exhibit nonlinear patterns, it indicates that the error distribution is not stable or that the linear relationship is failing to hold consistently across the different temporal segments.

    For Rolling LS, the sample quantiles are derived from the residuals calculated at each point \(t\) relative to its specific rolling window: $$Q(p) \text{ of } \{e_{t, \text{roll}}\} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \Phi^{-1}(p)$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the actual observed value for time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor vector for time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)

name: residual_vs_fitted.html
content:
Residual versus Fitted Plot: A Residual versus Fitted plot is a diagnostic visualization where the residuals (\( \epsilon \)) are plotted on the vertical axis against the predicted values (\( \hat{y} \)) on the horizontal axis. This plot is essential for detecting non-linearity, unequal error variances, and influential outliers.

In a correctly specified model, the residuals should be distributed as a random scatter around the horizontal zero line, showing no discernible trend or systematic change in spread.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    A curved trend in the points indicates that the model has failed to capture a non-linear relationship. If the vertical spread of the residuals increases or decreases with the fitted values (creating a funnel shape), it signifies heteroskedasticity, violating the OLS assumption of constant variance.

    Standard residuals are used to detect non-linearity or outliers: $$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) (e.g., \(\beta_0\) + \(\beta_1 x_i\) + \(\dots\))


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    This plot should utilize standardized residuals that have been scaled by the weights. A successful WLS model will show a constant vertical spread across all fitted values. If the funnel shape persists, the weighting function is likely misspecified and has not adequately corrected for the non-constant variance.

    For diagnostics, whitened residuals are plotted against the fitted values to verify that weighting successfully stabilized the variance: $$\tilde{e}_i = \sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \hat{y}_i) \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_i$$ Where:
    • \(w_i\) is the weight for each observation \(i\)
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) (e.g., \(\beta_0\) + \(\beta_1 x_i\) + \(\dots\))


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    The residuals are typically transformed by the inverse of the estimated covariance matrix. A random distribution in this plot confirms that the specified correlation structure (such as autocorrelation) has been successfully neutralized. Any remaining patterns indicate that the assumed error structure does not match the actual data.

    Similar to WLS, GLS uses whitened residuals based on the Cholesky decomposition (\(C\)) of the error covariance matrix \(\Sigma \): $$\mathbf{e}_{wh} = C^{-1}(y - \hat{y}) \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}$$ Where:
    • \(C\) is the Cholesky decomposition such that \(CC^\top = \Sigma\)
    • \(y\) is the vector of observations
    • \(\hat{y}\) is the vector of predicted values


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    While RLS often focuses on time-series stability, a plot of one-step-ahead forecast errors against fitted values can reveal if the model's accuracy is biased at certain magnitudes. If residuals deviate significantly from zero as fitted values change, it suggests the parameters are not stable across the full range of the dependent variable.

    RLS focuses on recursive residuals (\(v_{t}\)), which are one-step-ahead prediction errors, plotted against the corresponding one-step predictions: $$v_t = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_{t|t-1}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients at time \(t-1\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot monitors the stability of the fit across moving windows. If the residual scatter remains centered and random across all windows, the model is considered robust. Trends or shifts in the residual spread relative to the fitted values in specific windows indicate that the underlying relationship is time-varying.

    Rolling residuals are calculated using the parameters estimated for each specific local window: $$e_{t, \text{roll}} = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_{t, \text{window}}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)

name: residual_vs_leverage.html
content:
Residual versus Leverage Plot: A Residual versus Leverage plot is a diagnostic tool used to identify influential observations that disproportionately impact the regression estimates. The horizontal axis represents leverage (the extent to which an observation's predictor values deviate from the mean), while the vertical axis represents standardized residuals. Points located in the extreme corners, often categorized by Cook's distance contours, indicate observations that possess both high leverage and large residuals, exerting significant influence on the model’s coefficients.

In a robust model, most observations should exhibit low leverage and small residuals, appearing clustered near the center-left of the plot.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    Observations with high leverage and large residuals can pull the regression line away from the general trend. These points warrant investigation to ensure they are not data entry errors or unique cases that necessitate a different model structure.

    Standardized residuals are calculated using the global RMSE and the leverage values extracted from the diagonal of the hat matrix: $$z_i = \frac{y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}}{\hat{\sigma} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_{ii}$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for OLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    This plot identifies whether certain weighted observations maintain undue influence despite the weighting scheme. If points with low weights still show significant influence in the standardized residual space, the weighting may not have sufficiently mitigated the impact of these outliers.

    Leverage in WLS is calculated using the weighted design matrix, and residuals are whitened by the user-defined weights: $$z_i = \frac{\sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_{ii}$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) under WLS
    • \(w_i\) is the user-defined weight for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    This diagnostic assesses influence within the context of the assumed covariance structure. Points that remain highly influential after transformation suggest that the specified correlation or heteroskedasticity structure does not adequately account for those specific data points.

    GLS uses the Cholesky factor (\(C\)) of the covariance matrix \(\Sigma \) to whiten both the residuals and the design matrix for leverage calculation: $$z_i = \frac{\mathbf{C}^{-1}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_{ii}$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) under GLS
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    ReLS utilizes recursive residuals to detect structural instability over time. An observation appearing with high influence may indicate a structural break, where a shift in the underlying process begins to dominate the recursive parameter estimates.

    While RLS diagnostics usually focus on cumulative sum (CUSUM) plots, one can plot standardized recursive residuals against the recursive leverage factor: $$w_t = \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}}{\sigma \sqrt{f_t}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_t$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients for the model at time \(t-1\)
    • \(\sigma\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(f_t\) is the recursive parameter estimate at time \(t\)
    • \(h_t\) is the recursive leverage at time \(t\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot identifies observations that are locally influential. A point might have minimal influence in a global model, but significant influence within a specific moving window, indicating that the relationship is particularly sensitive to that specific temporal data segment.

    The leverage for the "current" point in a rolling window is the leverage calculated relative only to the other points in that specific \(N\)-sized window: $$z_{t, \text{roll}} = \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{wndw}}}{\hat{\sigma}_{wndw} \sqrt{1 - h_{t,t}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad h_{t,t}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{wndw}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the model within the moving window
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wndw}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals within the moving window
    • \(h_{t,t}\) is the leverage for the current observation

name: residual_vs_predictor.html
content:
Residual versus Predictor Plot: A Residual versus Predictor plot is a diagnostic tool where residuals (\( \epsilon \)) are plotted on the y-axis against an independent variable (\(X\)) on the x-axis. The primary objective is to validate the assumption that the error terms are independent and identically distributed with a mean of zero.

The ideal outcome for any of the following models is a random scatter of points centered around the horizontal zero line, indicating no systematic relationship between the errors and the predictor.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    A curved pattern (e.g., U-shaped) suggests a non-linear relationship that the linear model failed to capture. A fan or megaphone shape indicates heteroskedasticity, meaning the variance of the errors is not constant across all levels of the predictor.

    This model uses standard unweighted residuals: $$e_i = y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) (e.g., \(\beta_0\) + \(\beta_1 x_i\) + \(\dots\))
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor matrix for observation \(i\)


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    The plot should utilize standardized (weighted) residuals. If the weighting scheme is correct, the non-constant variance observed in the OLS plot should disappear, resulting in a uniform horizontal band. Systematic patterns here suggest the chosen weights did not properly account for the variance structure.

    This model often uses weighted residuals: $$\tilde{e}_i = \sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{WLS})$$ Where:
    • \(w_i\) is the weight for each observation \(i\)
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{y}_i\) is the predicted value for observation \(i\) (e.g., \(\beta_0\) + \(\beta_1 x_i\) + \(\dots\))
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor matrix for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    This plot evaluates transformed residuals based on the assumed covariance matrix. Since GLS is used to handle autocorrelation or complex heteroskedasticity, a lack of pattern confirms that the covariance structure has been successfully modeled. Remaining clusters or trends suggest the model's error structure is misspecified.

    This model often focuses on normalized (whitened) residuals to account for the estimated error covariance matrix \(\Sigma \): $$\mathbf{e}_{whitened} = \mathbf{C}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\beta}_{GLS})$$ Where:
    • \(\mathbf{C}\) is the Cholesky decomposition such that \(\mathbf{CC}^\top = \Sigma\).
    • \(\mathbf{y}\) is the vector of observations
    • \(\mathbf{X}\) is the matrix of predictors
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{GLS}\) are the estimated coefficients


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    Recursive residuals are plotted to identify structural instability. In this context, if the residuals exceed the standard error bounds (calculated at each step), it provides evidence of a structural break or a shift in the coefficient values at specific points in the dataset.

    This model typically utilizes Recursive Residuals (\(v_{t}\)), which are the one-step-ahead prediction errors generated as the model window expands: $$v_t = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients at time \(t-1\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This diagnostic assesses how the model's error distribution evolves across moving windows. If the scatter remains random in certain windows but develops patterns in others, it indicates that the relationship between the predictor and the dependent variable is time-varying or contingent on specific data segments.

    This model's residuals are manually calculated using the parameters estimated for each local window: $$e_{t, \text{roll}} = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)

name: scale_location.html
content:
Scale-Location Plot: The Scale-Location plot, also referred to as the Spread-Location plot, displays the square root of the absolute standardized residuals on the vertical axis against the fitted values on the horizontal axis. This transformation reduces the skewness of the residuals, making it easier to evaluate the homoskedasticity assumption across the range of predictions.

A correctly specified model should produce a horizontal line with a random spread of points, suggesting that the error variance is constant.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    A discernible upward or downward trend in the points indicates heteroskedasticity. If the spread of the residuals increases with the magnitude of the fitted values, the OLS assumption of constant variance is violated, and the standard errors may be biased.

    Standardized residuals (\(z_{i}\)) are calculated by dividing raw residuals by their standard error and leverage: $$\sqrt{|z_i|} = \sqrt{\left| \frac{y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}}{\hat{\sigma} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \right|} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for OLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    Since WLS is specifically designed to address non-constant variance, the Scale-Location plot should ideally show a flat, horizontal trend. If a trend persists, it signifies that the weighting matrix was insufficient to stabilize the variance across the fitted values.

    For WLS, the residuals are "whitened" (weighted) using the square root of the user-defined weights before standardization: $$\sqrt{|z_i|} = \sqrt{\left| \frac{\sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{WLS})}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \right|} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{WLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for WLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    This plot evaluates whether the transformed residuals, adjusted for the assumed covariance structure, exhibit stable variance. A lack of a trend confirms that the model has successfully accounted for the specified variance-covariance relationships.

    GLS uses the Cholesky factor (\(C\)) of the error covariance matrix \(\Sigma \) to whiten the residuals: $$\sqrt{|z_i|} = \sqrt{\left| \frac{\mathbf{C}^{-1}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{GLS})}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \right|} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{GLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for GLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    While less common than time-series diagnostics, a Scale-Location plot for RLS can reveal if the forecast error variance changes as the predicted values grow. A non-horizontal line suggests that the model’s reliability fluctuates depending on the magnitude of the recursive estimates.

    Standardized recursive residuals (\(w_{t}\)) are based on the one-step-ahead forecast error and its variance \(f_{t}\): $$\sqrt{|w_t|} = \sqrt{\left| \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}}{\sigma \sqrt{f_t}} \right|} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_{t|t-1}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients at time \(t-1\)
    • \(\sigma\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(f_t\) is the forecast variance at time \(t\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot identifies whether the volatility of the residuals is consistent across different local windows. If certain segments of the fitted values show significantly higher vertical spread, it indicates that the relationship is subject to regime-dependent volatility or time-varying variance.

    The rolling version uses residuals, standard deviations, and leverage calculated within each moving window: $$\sqrt{|z_{t, \text{roll}}|} = \sqrt{\left| \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}}{\hat{\sigma}_{wndw} \sqrt{1 - h_{t,t}}} \right|} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \hat{y}_{t, \text{window}}$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wndw}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals for the window
    • \(h_{t,t}\) is the leverage for observation \(t\) in the window

name: stability.html
content:
Stability Plot: A Stability plot generally refers to visualizations used to detect structural breaks or parameter instability over time. While standard static models often rely on diagnostic tests for stability, time-varying models like Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) provide dedicated plotting methods to visualize how model coefficients or cumulative errors evolve as new data is added

The objective of these plots is to confirm the null hypothesis (\(H_0\)) that the regression parameters are constant across the entire sample. Stability is typically assessed using residual-based diagnostics like the CUSUM test. A significant deviation from zero in the cumulative sum of residuals indicates that the model's coefficients may have shifted at a specific point in the data.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    N/A. Static models do not inherently produce a stability plot of their parameters since coefficients are estimated once for the entire dataset.

  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    N/A. Static models do not inherently produce a stability plot of their parameters since coefficients are estimated once for the entire dataset.

  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    N/A. Static models do not inherently produce a stability plot of their parameters since coefficients are estimated once for the entire dataset.

  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    The plot includes significance bands (typically at the 5% level). If the CUSUM line crosses these boundaries, it suggests a structural break or parameter instability. Additionally, plotting recursive coefficients allows for observing if estimates stabilize as the sample size increases.

    The CUSUM plot tracks the cumulative sum of standardized recursive residuals. If the sum wanders outside a specific confidence band (typically 5%), the null hypothesis of parameter stability is rejected: $$W_t = \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}} \sum_{j=k+1}^{t} w_j \quad \text{vs.} \quad t$$ Where:
    • \(w_j\) is the standardized recursive residual for observation \(j\)
    • \(\hat{\sigma}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the recursive residuals


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    The plot displays the evolving coefficients along with 95% point-wise confidence intervals. Stability is interpreted by the constancy of the coefficient line; large fluctuations or trends in the rolling estimates indicate that the relationship between the predictor and the dependent variable is time-varying rather than stable.

    Rolling LS diagnostics focus on the Parameter Path. Stability is visually assessed by checking if the coefficient value \(\hat{\beta }_{t}\) stays constant or drifts significantly over time: $$\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}} = (\mathbf{X}_{t,n}^\top \mathbf{X}_{t,n})^{-1} \mathbf{X}_{t,n}^\top \mathbf{y}_{t,n} \quad \text{vs.} \quad t$$ Where:
    • \(\mathbf{X}_{t,n}\) is the predictor matrix for observations \(t\) to \(t+n-1\)
    • \(\mathbf{y}_{t,n}\) is the response vector for observations \(t\) to \(t+n-1\)

name: standardized_residuals_vs_predictor.html
content:
Standardized Residuals versus Predictor Plot: A Standardized Residuals versus Predictor plot displays the residuals, scaled by their estimated standard deviation, on the vertical axis against an independent variable on the horizontal axis. Standardizing the residuals allows for a more consistent evaluation of outliers and variance, as the resulting values typically follow a standard normal distribution if the model assumptions are met.

For all models, a well-specified relationship should result in a random scatter where approximately 95% of the points fall within the interval of -2 to +2 on the vertical axis.

  • Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Regression:
    The plot is used to identify heteroskedasticity and non-linearity. Because the residuals are standardized, observations exceeding 3 or falling below -3 are easily identified as influential outliers. A non-random pattern, such as a bow-tie or cone shape, indicates that the error variance is tied to the predictor.

    Standardized residuals (internally studentized) are used to detect outliers while accounting for the leverage of each data point: $$z_i = \frac{y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{OLS}}{\hat{\sigma} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for OLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression:
    This plot confirms whether the weighting has successfully achieved homoskedasticity. If the model is correct, the standardized residuals should show a constant vertical spread regardless of the predictor’s value. Persistent patterns suggest the weights were incorrectly calculated or that the variance relationship is misspecified.

    n WLS, the "standardized" residual is derived from the whitened (weighted) residual. It accounts for both the user-defined weights \(w_{i}\) and the leverage: $$z_i = \frac{\sqrt{w_i}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{WLS})}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{WLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for WLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Generalized Least Squares (GLS) Regression:
    The standardized residuals in GLS are adjusted for the autocorrelation or complex variance structure defined in the model. A random scatter indicates the covariance matrix has successfully captured the error dynamics. Any remaining clusters or trends imply that the residuals still contain systematic information.

    GLS uses residuals that have been whitened by the Cholesky factor \(C\) of the covariance matrix \(\Sigma \): $$z_i = \frac{\mathbf{C}^{-1}(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \hat{\beta}_{GLS})}{\hat{\sigma}_{wh} \sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_i$$ Where:
    • \(y_i\) is the actual observed value for observation \(i\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is the predictor vector for observation \(i\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{GLS}\) are the estimated coefficients for GLS regression
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wh}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the whitened residuals
    • \(h_{ii}\) is the leverage for observation \(i\)


  • Recursive Least Squares (ReLS) Regression:
    In the context of RLS, these are often standardized recursive residuals. If the model parameters are stable, these residuals should behave like white noise. If the residuals consistently drift outside the standard range as the predictor (often time) progresses, it suggests a structural break or parameter instability.

    Standardized recursive residuals are the "one-step-ahead" prediction errors divided by their forecast standard deviation: $$w_t = \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t-1}}{\sigma \sqrt{f_t}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_t$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t-1}\) are the estimated coefficients at time \(t-1\)
    • \(\sigma\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals
    • \(f_t\) is the forecast variance at time \(t\)


  • Rolling Least Squares (RoLS) Regression:
    This plot assists in identifying periods where the model's predictive power degrades. If the standardized residuals exhibit a wider spread or systematic bias during specific segments of the predictor range, it indicates that the relationship between the variables is not constant and may require a different window size or model specification.

    Since these are calculated in local windows, the residual is standardized using the standard deviation and leverage specific to that moving window: $$z_{t, \text{roll}} = \frac{y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}}{\hat{\sigma}_{wndw} \sqrt{1 - h_{t,t}}} \quad \text{vs.} \quad X_t$$ Where:
    • \(y_t\) is the response variable at time \(t\)
    • \(\mathbf{x}_t\) is the predictor matrix at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\beta}_{t, \text{window}}\) are the estimated coefficients for the window at time \(t\)
    • \(\hat{\sigma}_{wndw}\) is the estimated standard deviation of the residuals for the window
    • \(h_{t,t}\) is the leverage for observation \(t\) in the window
